room.name: the East End of Vauxhall Bridge.
room.description: The bridge descends toward the southern bank, where the neat avenues of Whitehall give way to industrial yards and abandoned warehouses. The air grows thicker with soot and the metallic tang of the river. Some way ahead, two guards in dark helmets and green overalls stand by a sandbagged checkpoint, their rifles slung lazily but their eyes sharp. A sign overhead reads: "RESTRICTED ZONE - AUTHORISED PERSONNEL ONLY." Beyond the barrier, the cracked spire of an old theatre looms above the rooftops, its faded billboard half-obscured by scaffolding and grime.

room.name: outside the Old Theatre.
room.description: A cracked forecourt opens before the theatre's soot-streaked facade. The gilt lettering above the main doors still faintly reads "Vauxhall Palace of Varieties". Two of the doors hang open, warped and splintered, revealing darkness beyond. The only light comes from a flickering streetlamp and the dull orange glow of a brazier nearby, where a few proles crouch in silence toasting stale bread. The theatre appears dark, cold and empty, the antithesis of the welcoming warmth it provided in its heyday.

room.name: a patrol stop.
room.description: The buildings on this part of the street have all been converted into military barracks. They line either side of the road. A tank is parked in the middle of the road, the barrel of its gun pointing back West towards the river. A barrier behind the tank is lowered. Guards with snipers rifles have been watching your approach. Guns are raised as you near. To the East lie a number of factories and manufacturing hubs.

room.name: the theatre foyer.
room.description: Faded theatre posters adorn the walls and an empty box office at the back. A staircase leads down to the theatre hall on one side, a locked door on the other.

room.name: the theatre.
room.description: A darkened basement theatre. Dusty floors and black mould on the walls are now all that remains to echo the forgotten monoloogues of the Golden Age. A dim bulb hangs over a makeshift dais - a table made from a door on bricks. A few rows of plastic chairs make up the audience section, more stacked against the walls. A few dozen proles are already gathered, more seem to be arriving.
